text: The surgeon identifies the left ureter, observing its vermiculation, and verifies its normal anatomical position. The lateral attachments are reduced to mobilize the sigmoid colon, followed by extending the mesenteric dissection distally towards the rectum.

The surgeon identifies the estimated point of transection at the coalescence of the colonic tinea and burrows a tunnel beneath the colon. The robotic third arm aids in exposure as the mesentery is dissected proximally using a vessel sealing device until reaching disease-free colon. The vessel sealing device provides hemostasis, though a vascular stapler may be needed for high ligation or larger sigmoid artery.

The surgeon mobilizes the left colon to extend its length, facilitating the proximal colon's reach into the pelvis. The intravascular injection of endocyanin green is activated, and the fluorescence lens of the da Vinci robot provides direct visualization of the colonic blood supply.

The surgeon accurately identifies the point of transection and uses two staple loads for the distal transection. If the proximal transection is incomplete, scissors are used to cut it, as the staple line will be removed in the next step. The specimen is freed and set aside. After removing the staple line, it is discarded into the colonic lumen. The pre-placed suture is removed from the anvil, and the anvil is placed into the proximal colon.

The surgeon creates a purse string suture at the colonic opening in preparation for anastomosis. The EEA stapler is inserted into the rectum, and its spike is deployed. The stapler and anvil are joined to form the anastomosis. The colon proximal to the anastomosis is occluded, and the pelvis is filled with saline to perform a leak test. Silk interrupted sutures are used to over-sew the staple line.
The surgeon removes the needles and suture material, followed by the extraction of the specimen from the surgical field.
